coredata.dc:description: Salicylic acid (SA), an essential endogenous phytohormone, plays a critical role in plant immunity by regulating stomatal responses under biotic and abiotic stresses. Accurate and real-time monitoring of SA accumulation and dynamics is essential for elucidating its physiological and pathological roles in systemic acquired resistance (SAR), agricultural productivity, and food security. Here, we synthesized a non-invasive and dual-channel ratiometric fluorescent probe RHSY@N-GQDs by coupling nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with RHSY. SA addition enhances π-π stacking interactions within the macrocyclic aromatic structure of RHSY@N-GQDs, while combined with hydrogen bond, synergistically drive probe aggregation, enabling an ultrafast response to SA (<1 s). Additionally, the probe exhibits excellent stability, broad pH tolerance, and ratiometric fluorescence properties, effectively eliminating interference from plant autofluorescence and the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect of RHSY and for real-time monitoring of SA in plants. Our probe provides a novel approach for monitoring SA dynamics in various plants and offers a promising tool for studying SA accumulation in subcellular microenvironments.

Characterization of RHSY@N-GQDs. (A) Synthesis route scheme of RHSY@N-GQDs; (B) TEM image of RHSY@N-GQDs, (inset: the particle diameter distribution of RHSY@N-GQDs); The high-resolution XPS spectra (C) C1s, (D) N1s, (E) O1s and (F) S2p of RHSY@N-GQDs; (G) FTIR spectrum of the GO, N-GQDs and RHSY@N-GQDs. (H) SEM images of RHSY and (I) RHSY@N-GQDs (scale bar = 2 μm) and (J) RHSY@N-GQDs with salicylic acid (scale bar = 5 μm).

(A) Scheme of RHSY@N-GQDs with or without SA. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra recorded during the titration of RHSY@N-GQDs with SA (from 0 to 200 μM) in a mixed solution. (C) Job’s plot analysis for determining the stoichiometry of RHSY@N-GQDs with SA. (D) Fluorescence response of RHSY@N-GQDs after adding 50 μM SA and its analogues. (E) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity (at 560 nm) of RHSY@N-GQDs with SA (from 10 to 20 min) and (inset: time-trace fluorescence photographs of RHSY@N-GQDs with SA (from 0 to 3 s)). (F) Fluorescence intensity of the RHSY@N-GQDs in the absence or presence of SA at pH 5–8. (G) Anti-interference capacity of RHSY@N-GQDs with various SA analogues and plant hormones.

(A) Chemical structure of RHSY and SA; (B) Partial 1H NMR spectra for different molar ratios of RHSY with SA (400 MHz, CDCl3); (C) The binding mechanisms of RHSY with SA, its analogs, and phytohormones. (distances in A).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salicylic acid (SA), an essential endogenous phytohormone, plays a critical role in plant immunity by regulating stomatal responses under biotic and abiotic stresses. Accurate and real-time monitoring of SA accumulation and dynamics is essential for elucidating its physiological and pathological roles in systemic acquired resistance (SAR), agricultural productivity, and food security. Here, we synthesized a non-invasive and dual-channel ratiometric fluorescent probe RHSY@N-GQDs by coupling nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with RHSY. SA addition enhances π-π stacking interactions within the macrocyclic aromatic structure of RHSY@N-GQDs, while combined with hydrogen bond, synergistically drive probe aggregation, enabling an ultrafast response to SA (<1 s). Additionally, the probe exhibits excellent stability, broad pH tolerance, and ratiometric fluorescence properties, effectively eliminating interference from plant autofluorescence and the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect of RHSY and for real-time monitoring of SA in plants. Our probe provides a novel approach for monitoring SA dynamics in various plants and offers a promising tool for studying SA accumulation in subcellular microenvironments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: During the growth process, plants have developed a series of defense mechanisms to counteract biotic and abiotic stresses, with the primary objective of preventing or minimizing damage [1,2]. Salicylic acid (SA), being a representative plant hormone, plays a pivotal role in activating plant resistance systems and lies at the core of the activation of such resistance systems as pattern-triggered immunity (PTI), effector-triggered immunity (ETI), and systemic acquired resistance (SAR) [3–6]. In 1979, White discovered that SA was the main component responsible for inducing resistance against tobacco mosaic virus [7] while prompting extensive investigations into how SA mediates defenses against viral, fungal, and bacterial pathogens [8–13]. Moreover, SA also regulates stomatal closure to thwart pathogen entry and conserve water [14,15], an immediate immune response that has broader significance in both biotic and abiotic stress tolerance [16]. Consequently, the dynamic monitoring of SA within the subcellular environment is of crucial importance for deepening our understanding of its physiological and pathological functions in the context of SAR mechanisms, agricultural production, and food security [16,17], as well as for facilitating the development of immune activators for crop protection.

Fluorescent molecular probes and electrochemical sensors are the two principal techniques for real-time SA detection. Over the past decade, fluorescence-based molecular probes have emerged as powerful tools for SA detection, offering rapid, non-invasive, and high-sensitivity monitoring. For example, in 2014, Ahmad et al. introduced an imidazole-based receptor modified with pyrene that effectively distinguishes SA from benzoic acid [18]. Later, Kim et al. employed hydrogen bonds, π-π stacking, and NH-π interactions between 5-aminosalicylic acid and a perylene moiety to achieve selective SA recognition [19]. In 2020, Yang et al. reported a rhodamine-based probe that relies on hydrogen bonding to open its spirolactam ring upon interaction with SA, yielding strong fluorescence signals [20]. Compared with electrochemical sensors that depend on microelectrodes to determine specific analytes by quantifying current signals derived from catalytic oxidation reactions through advanced instrumentation for precise analysis [21,22], fluorescent molecular probes demonstrate superior operational efficiency. Recently, Ma et al. demonstrated that benzothiazole-rhodamine derivatives show high sensitivity and fast responses to SA in both callus tissue and pea roots [23], while Chen et al. demonstrated that heterocycles in rhodamine 6G frameworks accelerate SA detection and improve photostability [24]. Subsequently, Tan et al. further advanced the field with a FRET-based ratiometric probe, enabling real-time imaging of SA in living plants and validating SA-induced stomatal closure [25]. These probes can enter plants through leaf surfaces and roots, gradually expanding their application for SA detection across various biological systems. Despite these achievements, these fluorescent probes still face the challenge of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), especially when tracking SA in complex plant tissues, compromising detection sensitivity. Therefore, exploring fluorescent probes to avoid ACQ is essential while enabling in vivo SA detection in plants.

Quantum dots (QDs), as a class of nanomaterials discovered in the 1980 s, have attracted increasing attention for their unique optical properties [26–30]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely utilized in fluorescence-based sensing and bioimaging due to their low toxicity, aqueous dispersibility, and ease of functionalization [31–34]. In particular, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibit enhanced photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, surface area, and physicochemical stability as a result of nitrogen atom incorporation, which modulates the electronic structure [35,36,37]. For instance, Liu et al. developed biocompatible N-GQDs as effective two-photon fluorescence probes for cell and deep tissue imaging [36]. Fang et al. synthesized N-GQDs to detect Al3+ ions in actual samples [37]. Fu et al. demonstrated their suitability in vitro fluorescence bioimaging [38]. In addition, developing dual-emission fluorescent probes by doping GQDs with conventional fluorophore-based molecules has gained momentum to improve detection accuracy and overcome ACQ [39]. Zhao et al. invented a dual-emission two-photon fluorescence nanoprobe with a signal switch [40]. Li et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence composite nanomaterial for RNA detection using GQDs and fluorescence probe molecules [41]. However, most of the applications are focused on the environmental and medical fields, with limited attention given to the detection of plant hormones.

In this work, we synthesized green emission N-GQDs using a simple hydrothermal method and employed it as reference fluorophores to reduce interference from plant autofluorescence. Building on our previous work, we designed and synthesized RHSY, a rhodamine 6G derivative with a six-membered heterocyclic ring, as an SA-responsive fluorophore. By coupling RHSY with N-GQDs, the RHSY@N-GQDs probe was constructed, which maintains uniform dispersion to mitigate ACQ and exhibits favourable biocompatibility. The response of RHSY@N-GQDs to SA was evaluated using ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, confirming its high sensitivity and selectivity for SA detection. Notably, adding SA-induced aggregation of RHSY@N-GQDs achieves an ultrafast response to SA (< 1 s). Additionally, leaf surface monitoring exhibited a fluorescence shift from green to yellow, correlating with SA transport pathways in plants. The average stomatal aperture diameter decreased from approximately 9.11 μm to 3.71 μm following SA addition, offering the potential of this probe for the dynamic monitoring of SA in plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of RHSY, N-GQDs, and RHSY@N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the synthetic route of a rhodamine 6G derivative RHSY is shown in Scheme S1, a six-membered heterocyclic ring that has been synthesized through an acyl chloride reaction. The product was satisfactorily characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, HRMS, and X-ray crystallography ( Figs. S1–S4 ). Subsequently, N-GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using graphene oxide as the precursor (Fig. 1
A and 1G) [36,37]. The N-GQDs exhibited particle sizes ranging from 0.70 to 8.87 nm, with an average size of approximately 4.27 nm (Fig. 1 C). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.276 nm (inset of Fig. 1 B), consistent with the (020) plane of graphite, indicating the presence of graphite crystal nuclei [38]. The full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra showed that the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s of N-GQDs were located at 284.4 eV, 400.15 eV, and 531.4 eV ( Fig. S20 ), respectively. In the high-resolution C1s spectrum, peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.48 eV, and 288.48 eV are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbon (C–C/C=C), (C=O/C–O), and C–N bonds, respectively, which are in agreement with literature reports [36,37]. The two fitted peaks at 531.97 and 531.04 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1 E) are assigned to C–OH and C=O groups, respectively [37,42]. Furthermore, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1 F) exhibits two peaks at 399.38 eV and 401.58 eV, corresponding to the C–N and N–(C)3 bonds, respectively [36,37,42]. These assignments confirm that nitrogen atoms have been successfully doped into the GQDs. FT-IR further confirmed the functional groups present ( Fig. S13 ). Compared with the GO, the N-GQDs lacked the epoxy band at 1064 cm−1, with new peaks at 1123 cm−1 and 2936 cm−1, which were attributed to the C–N–C and C–H asymmetric stretching of dimethylformamide ( Fig. S19 ) [36].

The RHSY@N-GQDs probe was synthesized via a coupling reaction between the carboxyl groups of N-GQDs and the amino groups of RHSY (Fig. 2
A) [43,44,45]. The crude product was purified by dialysis with a 1000 Da dialysis bag and washed three times with a water/ethanol (1/1) mixture to obtain the pure final product of RHSY@N-GQDs. To confirm the successful preparation of RHSY@N-GQDs, TEM, XPS, FTIR, EDS, and SEM were utilized for characterization. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2 B, the TEM display particle of RHSY@N-GQDs was approximately 89.83 nm, significantly more significant than the ungrafted N-GQDs. The full-scan XPS spectra showed that the binding energies of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p are 285.32 eV, 399 eV, 532.13 eV, and 164 eV, respectively ( Figs. S20 ). Compared to N-GQDs, RHSY@N-GQDs exhibited sulfur and nitrogen content and decreased oxygen content ( Fig. S19 ), which is presumed to be attributed to the grafting of RHSY. High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2 C) indicated the presence of sp2 carbon (C=C/C–C, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbon (C–N/C–S, 285.5 eV), and oxygenated carbon (C=O/C–O, 287.2 eV) [43,46,47,48], indicating the presence of functional groups from RHSY. Similarly, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2 D) revealed peaks corresponding to amino–N (397.9 eV), pyridinic–N (398.99 eV), pyrrolic–N (399.5 eV), and amide–N (400.54 eV) [46,48,49], further confirming successful incorporation of RHSY. Besides, the O1s spectrum displayed peaks at 531.21 eV, 532.23 eV, and 533.27 eV, attributed to O–H, C=O, and C–O groups (Fig. 2 E) [50,51]. FTIR analysis identified characteristic bands at 3255 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 (Fig. 2 G), corresponding to amino and amide groups, verifying the formation of amide bonds [42,43,51]. As shown in Fig. S6
A, the peak at 167.7 ppm in RHSY@N-GQDs corresponds to the carbonyl group (–CO–NH–) [48], while the peak at 45.5 ppm is assigned to the methylene group adjacent to a tertiary amine, originating from the functional groups on RHSY. The chemical shifts in the 14.8–29.6 ppm ranges, and 38.3–59.1 ppm are attributed to aliphatic alkyl carbons and carbons near amine groups [48,52], respectively. In Fig. S6
B, the resonance at 2.21 ppm is ascribed to methyl groups proximal to amine functionalities, resulting from dimethylamine (DMF) doping on GQDs [36,37]. Collectively, these results conclusively confirm the successful formation of amide bonds. This amide bond formation effectively transitioned the fluorescence of N-GQDs from a quenched emission to an anti-quenching emission. The sulfur signature detected via EDS elemental mapping is conclusively assigned to RHSY ( Fig. S5 ). Additionally, potentiometric analyses further supported the successful synthesis. The zeta potential of N-GQDs in deionized water was −18.62 mV, attributed to surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, while the zeta potential changed to +1.72 mV after grafting with RHSY ( Fig. S24 ) [53]. Moreover, using the standard curve, the amount of RHSY grafted onto N-GQDs was calculated to be 0.27 mg/mg ( Fig. S8 ). Besides, after grafting with N-GQDs, the particle size of RHSY@N-GQDs decreased compared to RHSY (Fig. 2 H and 2I), and this was consistent with the particle size measured by TEM. It has been proven that the irregular structure of N-GQDs functions as a spacer between RHSY, reducing the ACQ effect by preventing close packing of RHSY, thereby enhancing dispersibility [39,54]. As illustrated in Fig. S22 (D–G), the RHSY@N-GQDs system exhibited significant RMSD fluctuations (>10 ns) before stabilization, followed by pronounced conformational rearrangements, indicating unstable structural dynamics throughout the simulation. Combined with Gyrate analysis, RHSY@N-GQDs exhibited aggregation instability. Negligible intermolecular interactions (∼0 kJ/mol) were observed in RHSY@N-GQDs, confirming a non-aggregated molecular dispersion. RHSY@N-GQDs exhibited partial solvent accessible surface area reduction followed by near-complete recovery to baseline levels, consistent with homogeneous molecular dispersion and absence of aggregation. The data from the molecular dynamics (MD) simulations further substantiate that RHSY@N-GQDs exhibit superior dispersibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of RHSY@N-GQDs probe for SA
ce:section.ce:para:
The photophysical properties of N-GQDs, RHSY, and RHSY@N-GQDs were systematically investigated to assess their potential for detecting SA. The N-GQDs, serving as a reference fluorophore, showed no response to SA ( Fig. S9 ). In contrast, RHSY demonstrated high selectivity for SA ( Figs. S10, S11 and S12 ). Upon grafting onto N-GQDs, the RHSY@N-GQDs ( Figs. S12, S13
and 14) retained this specificity, responding strongly to SA while showing negligible anti-interference from related compounds (Fig. 3
D, G and Fig. S26 ), including ASA (acetylsalicylic acid), BA (benzoic acid), 4-OHBA (4-hydroxybenzoic acid), 2-MeBA (2-methylbenzoic acid), 2-MeOBA (2-methoxybenzoic acid), MeSA (methyl salicylate) and phenol, plant hormones such as 6-BAP (6-benzylaminopurine), ABA (abscisic acid), IAA (indoleacetic acid), NAA (1-Naphthaleneacetic acid) and GA (gibberellins). Notably, ( Fig. S14 ) the UV–vis analysis of RHSY@N-GQDs showed a distinct absorption peak at 534 nm, consistent with the characteristic absorption of RHSY, confirming that RHSY serves as the active fluorophore in the system. Fluorescence revealed that the green fluorescence at 495 nm was emitted without SA due to N-GQDs. Upon adding SA, the spirolactam ring of RHSY opened, producing dual emissions at 495 nm (N-GQDs) and 560 nm (RHSY). This enabled precise ratiometric detection of SA (Fig. 3 A and 3B). The shift in fluorescence from green to yellow provided a clear and observable signal of SA levels. Fig. S13
A demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of the probe RHSY@N-GQDs at 560 nm changes significantly in response to different SA concentrations, with peak intensity increasing as SA levels rise. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity ratio of RHSY@N-GQDs exhibits a positive linear correlation within the SA concentration range of 125–225 μM ( Fig. S13
B).

Job's plot determined the stoichiometric ratio between RHSY@N-GQDs and SA. The fluorescence intensity reached its maximum at a concentration ratio of SA to the total of SA and RHSY@N-GQDs of 0.5. This result indicated a 1:1 stoichiometric interaction between RHSY@N-GQDs and SA (Fig. 3 C). To evaluate the time response and stability in SA detection were examined. As shown in Fig. 3 E, the fluorescence intensity remained stable after multiple excitation cycles. Upon adding SA, the probe demonstrated a robust response and stability. Despite containing only 0.27 mg/mg of RHSY, the reaction time between RHSY@N-GQDs and SA was determined to be less than 1 s. This ultrafast response was attributed to the SA-induced aggregation of RHSY@N-GQDs, confirming the probe ability to detect SA efficiently and promptly. As illustrated in Fig. S18 , the fluorescence intensity exhibited marked amplification upon incremental SA addition, demonstrating a concentration-dependent response. These findings suggest the probe's potential utility for quantitative SA detection in systems attributable to its tunable fluorescence modulation upon analyte binding.

During real-sample detection, temporal, thermal, pH, and matrix interference factors may critically interfere with detection accuracy. A series of methodically designed experiments were conducted to comprehensively assess the environmental stability of RHSY@N-GQDs under simulated real-world conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S15 , the fluorescence emission of the probe ranges from 0 °C to 70 °C. The system maintained consistent signal responsiveness irrespective of thermal variance upon introducing SA. Temporal stability assays (≤1800 s post-SA administration) revealed the fluorescence signal remains stable over time ( Fig. S17 ). Also, the common chemicals in plants, such as ions including Cl−, Cd2+, Na+, K+, and NO3 2
- , do not hinder the probe’s ability to detect SA ( Fig. S16 ), indicating its strong anti-interference properties selectively. Additionally, a pH spectral response test was conducted to address concerns regarding the influence of pH on the probe performance in SA detection. Considering that the physiological pH of most plants ranges from 5 to 8, this range was selected for evaluation. As shown in Fig. 3 F, RHSY@N-GQDs exhibited excellent stability within this pH range. Following the addition of SA, the fluorescence intensity of RHSY@N-GQDs significantly increased. These results confirm that RHSY@N-GQDs demonstrate high stability and robust pH tolerance, making them suitable for SA detection in complex environmental conditions. Further, the biocompatibility of RHSY@N-GQDs was assessed via the CCK-8 cell viability assay [55,56,57]. After 24 h of incubation at concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg mL−1, no significant cytotoxicity was observed in A549 cells ( Fig. S27 ), indicating excellent biocompatibility under the tested conditions [55,57]. To further investigate the biocompatibility of RHSY@N-GQDs, we examined the germination and growth of bean sprouts under incubation. Compared to the control group, no phytotoxicity was observed in the sprouting process across varying concentrations of RHSY@N-GQDs ( Fig. S28 ), demonstrating the probe’s excellent biocompatibility [58,59]. These results confirm that RHSY@N-GQDs is a highly selective, rapidly responsive sensor for SA detection and its potential for real-time SA detection in plants. It offers a reliable tool for monitoring plant physiological processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RHSY and RHSY@N-GQDs response mechanism for SA
ce:section.ce:para:
To better understand the possible mechanism. Firstly, dynamic tracing of 1H NMR spectroscopy of RHSY in CDCl3 solution was performed with different ratios SA. The addition of SA influenced the chemical shifts of benzene ring protons (Fig. 4
B); at 0.5 equivalents of SA, the chemical shift variations for protons 1*, 2*, 3*, and 4* were −0.024, −0.034, −0.031, and −0.024 ppm, respectively. While at two equivalents of SA, the shifts were −0.006, −0.016, −0.017, and −0.011 ppm ( Table S2 ). This decrease suggests that binding sites on RHSY lead to a shielding effect as SA concentration increases. Further analysis revealed that proton 7 exhibited a more significant downfield shift than protons 1–5 ( Table S3 ), indicating a strong interaction with SA. As the concentration of SA increased, most protons on RHSY gradually displayed more tremendous chemical shift changes. Notably, proton 1 showed a pronounced downfield shift compared to protons 2 and 3, while protons at positions 9 and 10 exhibited upfield shifts. The proton signal at position 8 of RHSY disappeared upon binding with SA, which can be attributed to the shielding effect induced by the N–H … O hydrogen bonding between SA and RHSY. As previously reported, these phenomena are attributed to the opening of the six-membered spirolactam ring induced by SA [23]. Simultaneously, the protons on the SA benzene ring shifted upfield upon interaction with RHSY, likely due to the formation of N–H … O hydrogen bonds and π-π interactions. In contrast, protons at positions 4, 6, and 7 of RHSY shifted downfield, which may result from π-π interactions between the electron-rich benzene ring of SA and the planar benzothiazole moiety of RHSY. Based on Job's plot analysis and the experimental results mentioned above, we constructed a structural model of the RHSY-SA complex and analyzed its weak interactions and binding energy. As illustrated in Fig. 4 C, RHSY and SA can form two N–H … O interactions (1.867/1.714 Å) and one π-π interaction (4.035 Å), resulting in a distinct change in fluorescence emission potential.

To further validate the selective response of RHSY to SA, we also investigated the interactions between RHSY and various SA analogs, including ASA, BA, 4-OHBA, NAA, and 6-BAP, using DFT calculations. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the binding energies for RHSY + BA, RHSY + 4-OHBA, RHSY + ASA, RHSY + NAA, and RHSY + 6-BAP were –22.68 kcal/mol, −21.94 kcal/mol, −19.09 kcal/mol, −15.37 kcal/mol, and −9.6 kcal/mol, respectively. These values are all higher than RHSY + SA (−25.07 kcal/mol), indicating a stronger interaction between RHSY and SA. In contrast, SA analogs and phytohormones (BA, 4-OHBA, ASA, NAA, 6-BAP) form only one N–H … O (1.736–2.326 Å) or C–H … π (2.437 Å) interaction, as well as a π-π interaction (4.660 Å) with RHSY. Therefore, compared to SA analogs and other phytohormones, RHSY forms a more stable complex with SA through N–H … O and π-π interactions, which contributes to the specific selectivity of RHSY for SA.

The SEM revealed that the particle size of RHSY@N-GQDs increased notably upon adding SA compared to the size of the probe alone (Fig. 2 H, 2I, and 2 J). This observation suggests that SA induces the aggregation of RHSY@N-GQDs (Fig. 2 J and Fig. S25 ). EDS analysis delineates a substantial particle size increase upon SA incorporation, corroborating the aggregation mechanism triggered by SA ( Fig. S7 ). The aggregation is likely driven by π-π interactions between the conjugated structures of RHSY@N-GQDs (Fig. 4 C), as well as electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged SA molecules and positively charged RHSY@N-GQDs [50,55]. Despite 6-BAP exhibiting π-π stacking interactions with RHSY (Fig. 4 C), the distance is longer than RHSY + SA, and the absence of hydrogen bonds collectively proves insufficient to induce spirolactam ring-opening of RHSY. The ultrafast response time (<1 s) observed in vitro also corroborates the SA-induced aggregation for real-time detection. Furthermore, to validate the detection mechanism of RHSY@N-GQDs, we employed N-GQDs, synthesized via a reported method with an identical preparation process [36,37], as a model to construct the RHSY@N-GQDs framework for molecular dynamics MD simulations. The MD simulations ( Fig. S22 ) demonstrated that the RHSY@N-GQDs rapidly aggregated and stabilized upon adding SA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting SA by laser confocal fluorescence microscopy in plants
ce:section.ce:para: Given the outstanding in vitro performance, the RHSY@N-GQDs probe capabilities of in-living plants were investigated. Bean sprouts are fast-growing plants with high transparency, simple structure, low cost, and ease of cultivation, making them suitable for observing and analyzing fluorescence-labeled signals in experiments [58,59]. In this work, the three-day-old bean sprouts were incubated in the RHSY@N-GQDs solution for 30 min, 60 min, and 120 min (Fig. 5 ), respectively. Fluorescence observations under a 365 nm UV lamp revealed that longer incubation times resulted in progressively stronger fluorescence signals. After thorough washing with deionized water, the sprouts were transferred to an SA solution and incubated for 10 mins. Following co-incubation, fluorescence at the root system shifted to yellow under UV illumination, indicating the interaction with SA. These findings demonstrate that RHSY@N-GQDs successfully penetrate plants through the root system and validate their potential as a nanoprobe for plant fluorescence imaging.

The above experiments demonstrated that RHSY@N-GQDs enable dual-channel plant imaging, providing a more practical alternative for studying SA-induced stomatal changes than fluorescent protein markers, gene knockout, and transgenic approaches. Fig. 8 shows pre-incubation with RHSY@N-GQDs results in distinct green fluorescence on the leaf surface and guard cells surrounding the stomata, with an average stomatal aperture of approximately 9.11 μm. Following further incubation with SA, the fluorescence shifts from green to yellow around the leaf surface and stomata, reducing the stomatal aperture to about 3.71 μm. This observation confirms that SA accumulation in plants triggers stomatal closure through a regulatory mechanism, enabling plants to protect themselves under stress conditions.

Fluorescence imaging demonstrated distinct green and yellow fluorescence on the leaf surface, allowing for more apparent detection of SA-induced stomatal changes while effectively reducing interference from plant autofluorescence. In a nutshell, the nanoprobe RHSY@N-GQDs facilitates the detection of SA in plants and shows excellent potential as an effective probe for investigating SA-induced stomatal movements, offering valuable insights into plant resistance mechanisms against pathogens. This innovative approach to SA detection in plants is anticipated to deepen our understanding of SA-related physiological and pathological functions, paving the way for discovering novel immune activators for crop protection.

In this work, a dual-channel ratiometric probe RHSY@N-GQDs was constructed by simply coupling N-GQDs with RHSY. This probe effectively overcomes the ACQ effect of RHSY. The addition of SA triggers enhanced π-π stacking interactions within the macrocyclic aromatic framework of RHSY@N-GQDs, while synergistic electrostatic attractions and hydrogen bonding further enhance probe aggregation upon SA binding, leading to an ultrafast fluorescence response (<1 s). This probe exhibits distinct green and yellow emission channels, enabling high-contrast imaging of SA in plant tissues by minimizing background interference from autofluorescence. Leveraging these properties, we achieved dynamic fluorescence imaging of SA-induced stomatal closure and real-time monitoring of SA distribution within the subcellular microenvironment of plant tissues. These results not only validate the probe’s sensitivity and specificity but also establish RHSY@N-GQDs as a robust and biocompatible tool for in vivo plant hormone analysis. We believe this probe offers promising opportunities for high-resolution imaging and rapid detection of SA in complex biological systems, with potential applications in plant physiology, environmental monitoring, and precision agriculture.

The morphology and size distribution of N-GQDs and RHSY@N-GQDs were characterized using a TEM. SEM was used to analyze RHSY, RHSY@N-GQDs, and RHSY@N-GQDs + SA morphology. XPS analysis was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument. The photophysical properties of N-GQDs, RHSY, and RHSY@N-GQDs were characterized by PTI QuantaMaster™ 8000 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, Canada) and UV-1900 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan). FT-IR spectrum was determined using a Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

DFT calculations were performed using the Gaussian 16 program [60]. The exchange–correlation functional adopted was B3LYP of the local meta-generalized gradient approximation (meta-GGA). Van der Waals interactions were considered using the DFT-D3 method. Complete relaxation was carried out for all geometry optimizations in the work, and the 6-31G(d) basis sets were used for all elements. The bulk solvent effect of water media was analyzed by applying the self-consistent reaction field (SCRF) method with the SMD [61] solvent model. Meanwhile, the visualization and analysis of molecular orbitals and electrostatic distribution were conducted using Multiwfn and VMD software [62,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamic simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Initial structure optimization and frequency calculation for all molecules (RHSY@N-GQDs, RHSY@N-GQDs, and SA) use the density functional theory (DFT) method with the B3LYP density functional and 6-31G basis set using the ORCA [64,65,66,67,68,69,70,71](version 6.0.1) program. Subsequently, the RESP charge and optimized structure were calculated using the Multiwfn [62,72] program. Ten RHSY@N-GQDs were randomly inserted into the 8*8*8 nm box and then solvated with water, while RHSY@N-GQDs were solvated with water after adding the substance SA. The initial system was subjected to energy minimization via the steepest descent algorithm for 50,000 steps. Subsequently, the system used a restrained pre-equilibrated under the NVT ensemble for 500 ps to raise the temperature to 298.15 K. Afterward, a 500 ps NPT restrained pre-equilibration was performed to adjust the pressure to 1 atm. Once the temperature and the pressure were stable, the restraints were removed to allow molecules self-assembly within the solvent environment. The system was maintained under constant pressure using the C-rescale coupling method, with a cutoff radius of 1.2 nm and a time step of 1 fs. The total simulation time was 100 ns. The gaff force field of molecules was generated using the sobtop [73] program. The solvent utilized the Amber ff19SB-ILDN [74] force field, and the tip3p model was applied for water molecules. The V-rescale thermostar and C-rescale barostat were employed to control the system temperature and pressure, respectively. Short-range electrostatic and van der Waals interactions were calculated with a 1.2 nm cut off, while long-range electrostatic interactions were computed using the particle mesh Ewald (PME) method. The LINCS algorithm constrained bonds involving hydrogen atoms, and periodic boundary conditions (PBC) were applied in all three simulation box dimensions. Molecular dynamics simulations were conducted using GROMACS [75].

All fluorescence-related measurements, including emission spectra, titration, Job’s plots, and competition experiments, were carried out on a PTI QuantaMaster 8000. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a TU-1900 spectrophotometer. Then, the probe of RHSY@N-GQDs (100 μg mL−1) and a certain amount of SA, its analogues, and plant hormones were added into their respective solutions for selectivity and competition experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of SA in the roots of cucumber and tomato seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Cucumber and tomato seeds were sown in planting pots and kept in a greenhouse daily after 7–8 days. Prior to experimentation, the seedlings were gently removed from the soil and thoroughly rinsed with deionized water to eliminate any remaining soil. The cleaned seedlings were immersed in 2 mL of RHSY@N-GQDs solution (100 μg mL−1) and incubated for 1 h at room temperature. Following this incubation, the seedlings were rinsed three times with deionized water to remove excess probe and then transferred into 2 mL of SA solution for an additional 10 min. After being rinsed again three times with deionized water, the roots were immediately imaged using a Zeiss LSM 900 confocal fluorescence microscope at λex = 488/561 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of SA in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Arabidopsis seedlings were carefully removed from their medium, washed thoroughly with deionized water, and then transferred into 2 mL of RHSY@N-GQDs solution (100 μg mL−1). The seedlings were incubated at room temperature for 2 h for adequate probe uptake. Afterward, the RHSY@N-GQDs solution was removed, and the seedlings were rinsed three times with deionized water to eliminate any excess probe. Next, the seedlings were immersed in 2 mL of SA solution (400 μM) for 10 min under the same conditions. Following this incubation, the seedlings were washed three times with deionized water and immediately imaged using a Zeiss LSM 900 confocal fluorescence microscope (λex = 488/561 nm). All imaging parameters were kept consistent across samples to ensure reliable comparisons.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of RHSY@N-GQDs and RHSY in live cells, 100 μL (1.0 × 104 cells mL−1) of A549 cells were first seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in CO2 incubator. The cells were then incubated with RHSY@N-GQDs and RHSY at different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 200 μg mL−1). Finally, cell viability after 24 h of incubation with RHSY@N-GQDs and RHSY was calculated using the CCK-8 method. After further incubation for 1 min in the dark, the absorbance was measured at 450 nm by a multi-well Microplate reader (BioTek Instrament, Inc., American). The study was carried out in triplicate for each concentration. The cell viability (%) was calculated according to the following equation:
Cellviability

OD450 (Sample) and OD450 (Control) are the sample's and control's absorbances, respectively, at 450 nm.

Uniform-sized mung beans were soaked in ultrapure water for 6 h, then transferred to Petri dishes containing graded concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg mL−1) of RHSY@N-GQDs solutions. The samples were incubated in a temperature-controlled growth chamber at 27°C. Germination progression was periodically monitored, and morphological changes were photographically documented at defined intervals to assess developmental responses to the treatments.
